tools.description: Create a new GitHub issue for tracking bugs, feature requests, or tasks. Use this for actionable work items that need assignment, labeling, and status tracking. For reports, announcements, or status updates that don't require task tracking, use create_discussion instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 5 issue(s) can be created. Title will be prefixed with "[improve] ". Labels ["automation" "improvement"] will be automatically added.

body.description: Detailed issue description in Markdown. Do NOT repeat the title as a heading since it already appears as the issue's h1. Include context, reproduction steps, or acceptance criteria as appropriate.

fields.description: Optional issue fields to set after creating the issue (e.g., Priority, Iteration, Start Date).

name.description: Issue field name exactly as configured in the repository (e.g., "Priority", "Iteration").

value.description: Field value. Use string for text, single-select, iteration, and date (YYYY-MM-DD) fields; use number for numeric fields.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

labels.description: Labels to categorize the issue (e.g., 'bug', 'enhancement'). Labels must exist in the repository.

parent.description: Parent issue number for creating sub-issues. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 42 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/42). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123', 'aw_Test123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

temporary_id.description: Unique temporary identifier for referencing this issue before it's created. Format: 'aw_' followed by 3 to 12 alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'aw_abc1', 'aw_Test123'). Use '#aw_ID' in body text to reference other issues by their temporary_id; these are replaced with actual issue numbers after creation.

title.description: Concise issue title summarizing the bug, feature, or task. The title appears as the main heading, so keep it brief and descriptive.

tools.name: create_issue

tools.description: Add a comment to an existing GitHub issue, pull request, or discussion. Use this to provide feedback, answer questions, or add information to an existing conversation. For creating new items, use create_issue, create_discussion, or create_pull_request instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 10 comment(s) can be added. Supports reply_to_id for discussion threading.

body.description: The comment text in Markdown format. This is the 'body' field - do not use 'comment_body' or other variations. Provide helpful, relevant information that adds value to the conversation.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: The issue, pull request, or discussion number to comment on. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 123 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/123). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run. If omitted, the tool will attempt to resolve the target from the current workflow context (triggering issue, PR, or discussion). If auto-targeting fails (e.g., for schedule or workflow_dispatch triggers), the tool call will fail with an error.

pr.description: Alias for item_number when targeting a pull request. Prefer item_number in new calls.

pr_number.description: Alias for item_number when targeting a pull request. Prefer item_number in new calls.

reply_to_id.description: Node ID of the discussion comment to reply to, enabling threaded discussion comments. When provided, the new comment is posted as a reply to the specified top-level discussion comment. If the given node ID belongs to a nested reply, the handler automatically resolves it to the top-level parent. Only applicable for discussion comments — ignored for issue and pull request comments.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

temporary_id.description: Unique temporary identifier for this comment. Format: 'aw_' followed by 3 to 12 alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'aw_abc1', 'aw_Test123'). Auto-generated if not provided. The temporary ID is returned in the tool response so you can reference this comment later.

tools.name: add_comment

tools.description: Create a new GitHub pull request to propose code changes. Use this after making file edits to submit them for review and merging. The PR will be created from the current branch with your committed changes. For code review comments on an existing PR, use create_pull_request_review_comment instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 1 pull request(s) can be created. Title will be prefixed with "[improve] ". Labels ["automation" "improvement"] will be automatically added. Reviewers ["kaovilai"] will be assigned.

base.description: Target base branch for the pull request. This override is only allowed when workflow configuration sets safe-outputs.create-pull-request.allowed-base-branches and the value matches one of those patterns.

body.description: Detailed PR description in Markdown. Include what changes were made, why, testing notes, and any breaking changes. Do NOT repeat the title as a heading.

branch.description: Source branch name containing the changes. If omitted, uses the current working branch.

draft.description: Whether to create the PR as a draft. Draft PRs cannot be merged until marked as ready for review. Use mark_pull_request_as_ready_for_review to convert a draft PR. Default: true.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

labels.description: Labels to categorize the PR (e.g., 'enhancement', 'bugfix'). Labels must exist in the repository.

repo.description: Target repository in 'owner/repo' format. Required when changes are in a subdirectory checkout (e.g., 'repos/repo-a/'). Must be in the allowed-repos list. If omitted, uses the repository at the workspace root.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

title.description: Concise PR title describing the changes. Follow repository conventions (e.g., conventional commits). The title appears as the main heading.

tools.name: create_pull_request

tools.description: Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because the required functionality is missing or access is restricted. When a bash command is blocked by security policy, call this tool with reason set to "security".

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this tool is needed or what information you want to share about the limitation (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tool.description: Optional: Name or description of the missing tool or capability (max 128 characters). Be specific about what functionality is needed.

tools.name: missing_tool

tools.description: Log a transparency message when no significant actions are needed. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

message.description: Status or completion message to log. Should explain what was analyzed and the outcome (e.g., 'Code review complete - no issues found', 'Analysis complete - all tests passing').

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: noop

tools.description: Report that data or information needed to complete the task is not available. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because required data, context, or information is missing.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

context.description: Additional context about the missing data or where it should come from (max 256 characters).

data_type.description: Type or description of the missing data or information (max 128 characters). Be specific about what data is needed.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: missing_data

tools.description: Signal that the task could not be completed due to an infrastructure or tool failure (e.g., MCP server crash, missing authentication, inaccessible repository). Use this when required tools or data are unavailable and the task cannot be meaningfully performed. This is distinct from noop (no action needed) — it indicates an active failure that prevented the task from running. The workflow framework will treat this as a failure signal even when the agent exits successfully.

details.description: Optional extended details or diagnostic context about the failure (max 65000 characters).

reason.description: A concise explanation of why the task could not be completed (max 1024 characters). Be specific about which tools or resources were unavailable.

tools.name: report_incomplete

1. get - Get details of a specific issue.
2. get_comments - Get issue comments.
3. get_sub_issues - Get sub-issues of the issue.
4. get_labels - Get labels assigned to the issue.

author.description: Author username or email address to filter commits by

path.description: Only commits containing this file path will be returned

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

sha.description: Commit SHA, branch or tag name to list commits of. If not provided, uses the default branch of the repository. If a commit SHA is provided, will list commits up to that SHA.

since.description: Only commits after this date will be returned (ISO 8601 format: YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ or YYYY-MM-DD)

until.description: Only commits before this date will be returned (ISO 8601 format: YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ or YYYY-MM-DD)

after.description: Cursor for pagination. Use the endCursor from the previous page's PageInfo for GraphQL APIs.

direction.description: Order direction. If provided, the 'orderBy' also needs to be provided.

orderBy.description: Order issues by field. If provided, the 'direction' also needs to be provided.

state.description: Filter by state, by default both open and closed issues are returned when not provided

annotations.title: Get details for a single pull request

tools.description: Get information on a specific pull request in GitHub repository.

method.description:
Action to specify what pull request data needs to be retrieved from GitHub. 
Possible options: 
 1. get - Get details of a specific pull request.
 2. get_diff - Get the diff of a pull request.
 3. get_status - Get combined commit status of a head commit in a pull request.
 4. get_files - Get the list of files changed in a pull request. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.
 5. get_review_comments - Get review threads on a pull request. Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews. Returns threads with metadata (isResolved, isOutdated, isCollapsed) and their associated comments. Use cursor-based pagination (perPage, after) to control results.
 6. get_reviews - Get the reviews on a pull request. When asked for review comments, use get_review_comments method.
 7. get_comments - Get comments on a pull request. Use this if user doesn't specifically want review comments. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.
 8. get_check_runs - Get check runs for the head commit of a pull request. Check runs are the individual CI/CD jobs and checks that run on the PR.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: pull_request_read

arguments.alternatives: Run `go build ./...` and `go vet ./...` manually in the terminal from the project directory.

arguments.body:
## Summary

Two code quality improvements to `pkg/cbt/cbt.go`:

### 1. Extract `newResultAndArgs` helper

All five public CBT methods (`GetAllocatedBlocks`, `GetChangedBlocks`, `GetChangedBlocksByID`, `GetAllocatedBlocksWithOptions`, `GetChangedBlocksWithOptions`) repeated the same boilerplate:

```go
result := &MetadataResult{}
emitter := &collectingEmitter{result: result}
args := iterator.Args{
    Clients:     c.clients,
    Emitter:     emitter,
    Namespace:   c.namespace,
    SANamespace: c.saNamespace,
    SAName:      c.saName,
}
```

This is now extracted into `(*Client).newResultAndArgs()`, which each method calls before setting only its snapshot-specific fields (`SnapshotName`, `PrevSnapshotName`, `PrevSnapshotID`, `StartingOffset`, `MaxResults`).

### 2. Remove debug log statements

- `GetAllocatedBlocks` had two `log.Printf("DEBUG ...")` calls inconsistently added during development.
- `collectingEmitter.SnapshotMetadataIteratorRecord` and `SnapshotMetadataIteratorDone` each had a `log.Printf("DEBUG ...")`.
- None of the other four public methods had any debug logging.

These are removed, along with the now-unused `"log"` import. The `SnapshotMetadataIteratorDone` parameter is now blank (`_ int`) since it had nothing to do.

**Result:** -39 lines, no behavior change.
arguments.title: pkg/cbt: reduce duplication with newResultAndArgs helper, remove debug logs
